coredata.dc:title: Sustainable WC-FeCrNiMo coatings by high-velocity air fuel technique: Corrosion and tribocorrosion performance compared to standard WC-CoCr system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gopal, Vasanth
Riche, Antonin
Narayanan, Uva
Kesavan, Praveenkumar
Björklund, Stefan
Lanz, Oliver
Bigdeli, Sedigheh
Manivasagam, Geetha
Joshi, Shrikant
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the corrosion and tribocorrosion behaviour of sustainable WC-FeCrNiMo coatings that use a Co-free binder, deposited via high-velocity air fuel (HVAF) process, and compared with standard WC-CoCr coatings. The effects of powder particle size and binder chemistry were systematically evaluated. All coatings displayed dense microstructures that maintained feedstock features, with higher deposition efficiency observed for coarser powders. XRD analysis showed minimal phase changes, with only slight decarburization. Electrochemical testing indicated that coatings made with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powder had better corrosion resistance, characterized by lower passivation current density (Ipp), a well-defined passivation plateau, and higher impedance. Tribocorrosion tests at both open circuit potential (OCP) and potentiostatic conditions showed stable electrochemical responses and a similar wear rate for coating deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powder. Overall, HVAF-sprayed WC-FeCrNiMo coatings, especially with fine powder feedstock, offer a promising, sustainable alternative to traditional Co-based WC coatings in applications requiring corrosion and wear resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: WC-FeCrNiMo
High Velocity Air Fuel (HVAF)
Corrosion
Tribocorrosion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The tribocorrosion test protocol a) OCP and b) potentiostatic.
Cross-sectional microstructure of lower and higher magnification of (a, a1) fine WC-FeCrNiMo, (b, b1) coarse WC-FeCrNiMo, (c, c1) fine WC-CoCr, and (d, d1) coarse WC-CoCr powder, respectively.
Cross-sectional morphology at low and higher magnification of (a-b) WC-Fe_Pf, (c-d) WC-Fe_Pc, (e-f) WC-CoCr_Pc coatings, (g-h) WC-CoCr_Pf, and i) thickness-deposition rate, respectively.
XRD patterns of a) WC-FeCrNiMo powder and coatings, and b) WC-CoCr powder and coating, respectively.
a) Potentiodynamic polarization plots and b) corrosion current densities (Ipp) of the coatings, respectively, as evaluated from the Tafel plot.
a) Nyquist, b) Bode impedance, c) Bode phase angle, and d) equivalent electrical circuit of all the coatings. The inset shows the raw and fitted data of WC-Fe_Pf, respectively.

Lower and higher magnification corroded morphology of (a, b) WC-Fe_Pf, (c, d) WC-Fe_Pc, (e, f) WC-CoCr_Pc, and (g, h) WC-CoCr_Pf coatings. The arrow in image (d) indicates extensive corrosion of the binder.
Open circuit potential of the coatings under the sliding motion.
Evolution of the current of all the coatings in the potentiostatic test.
Worn surface morphology of (a, b, c) WC-Fe_Pf, (d, e, f) WC-Fe_Pc and (g, h, i) WC-CoCr_Pc and (j, k, l) WC-CoCr_Pf coatings tested under potentiostatic conditions.
EDS mapping inside the wear track of (a) WC-Fe_Pf, (b) WC-Fe_Pc, (c) WC-CoCr_Pc, and (d) WC-CoCr_Pf coatings.
3D optical profilometer of wear track of a) WC-Fe_Pf, b) WC-Fe_Pc, c) WC-CoCr_Pc, d) WC-CoCr_Pf, and e) volume loss of the coatings tested under potentiostatic conditions.
HVAF process parameters adopted to deposit the coatings.

head.ce:title: Sustainable WC-FeCrNiMo coatings by high-velocity air fuel technique: Corrosion and tribocorrosion performance compared to standard WC-CoCr system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the corrosion and tribocorrosion behaviour of sustainable WC-FeCrNiMo coatings that use a Co-free binder, deposited via high-velocity air fuel (HVAF) process, and compared with standard WC-CoCr coatings. The effects of powder particle size and binder chemistry were systematically evaluated. All coatings displayed dense microstructures that maintained feedstock features, with higher deposition efficiency observed for coarser powders. XRD analysis showed minimal phase changes, with only slight decarburization. Electrochemical testing indicated that coatings made with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powder had better corrosion resistance, characterized by lower passivation current density (Ipp), a well-defined passivation plateau, and higher impedance. Tribocorrosion tests at both open circuit potential (OCP) and potentiostatic conditions showed stable electrochemical responses and a similar wear rate for coating deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powder. Overall, HVAF-sprayed WC-FeCrNiMo coatings, especially with fine powder feedstock, offer a promising, sustainable alternative to traditional Co-based WC coatings in applications requiring corrosion and wear resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Tungsten Carbide (WC)-based metal matrix coatings by thermal spraying are well-established to protect a wide spectrum of industrial components against wear [1,2]. The hard WC grains provide excellent wear resistance, while the softer metal serves as a binder, offering physical support to the carbides. This combination of hard carbides and soft binders contributes remarkable hardness and toughness, which in turn enhances the wear resistance [3–6]. Besides physical support, the choice of metal binders is crucial in determining the overall corrosion resistance of WC-metal binder-based coatings. For example, while conventional WC-12Co coatings are known for their outstanding wear resistance, their corrosion performance is still unsatisfactory. The addition of Cr to the Co metal binder has been shown to exhibit better corrosion resistance properties than without Cr [7,8]. For instance, the standard WC-10Co4Cr coating demonstrates better corrosion resistance due to its passivating ability and a smaller driving force for micro-galvanic corrosion than the WC-12Co coating [9,10]. This combination of excellent wear and better corrosion resistance has led to the widespread adoption of WC-10Co4Cr coating to protect components subject to wear and corrosion degradation. However, there have been growing concerns in recent times about utilizing Co-based binders due to their potential health hazards, as they are known to be toxic and listed as carcinogenic [11]. Besides health issues, there exists significant uncertainty in the supply chain and price of Co, which is listed as a Critical Raw Material (CRM) by the European Union [12].

The quest to find an alternative to Co-based metal binders has been gradually gathering importance within the thermal spray community, and there have been increasing efforts to develop sustainable Co-lean or Co-free WC-metal binder-based coatings. Fe- and Ni-based metal binders are considered to be the most promising alternative to Co-based binders. Testa et al. [13] investigated the sliding wear and corrosion performance of coatings utilizing three alternative metal binders, namely NiMoCrFeCo, FeNiCrMoCu, and FeCrAl, processed by high-velocity oxyfuel (HVOF) and compared them with the standard WC-10Co4Cr coating. They observed that WC-NiMoCrFeCo coatings exhibit better corrosion resistance than WC-10Co4Cr coatings, whereas the WC-FeCrAl coating shows slightly lower corrosion resistance. The authors’ group has also investigated the performance of WC-based coatings with the same NiMoCrFeCo, FeNiCrMoCu, and FeCrAl binders deposited by High-velocity air fuel (HVAF) spraying [14]. Although HVOF provides advantages over other thermal spray techniques, such as plasma spraying, it often results in some degree of decarburization of WC during the deposition processes. HVAF is a potential alternate technique to deposit WC-based coating due to its relatively lower combustion temperature and higher kinetic energy than HVOF, which helps to minimize undesired phase transformation during deposition and enhances adhesion strength. To harness these advantages of HVAF technology, WC-based coatings with the above-mentioned Fe- and Ni-based metal binders were developed and investigated for their corrosion performance. The results demonstrated that coatings sprayed with both Ni- and Fe-based binders exhibit better corrosion resistance than the WC-10Co4Cr coating [14]. In another work, Javed et al. assessed the corrosion performance of HVOF-sprayed WC-Hastelloy C (WCH) and WC-10Ni5Cr (WCN) coatings and compared them with atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) Al2O3-40TiO2 coatings. When subject to potentiodynamic polarization and salt spray tests, both WCH and WCN exhibited nobler potential and no sign of corrosion attack, respectively. Furthermore, these coatings showed delayed biofouling activity compared to APS coating, suggesting overall better corrosion performance [15].

Another critical challenge for the WC-based coatings is when they are exposed to dynamic service conditions involving synergistic action of wear and corrosion, referred to as “Tribocorrosion”. This form of degradation is prevalent in industries such as marine (propellers, shafts, valves), oil and gas (pumps and valves), and mining (slurry pumps and grinding mills), where the components are subject to wear and corrosion simultaneously. The most important aspect of tribocorrosion is that the degradation mechanism is highly complex as well as completely different from the mechanism involved in static corrosion and pure wear. For instance, the passive layer that forms on the surface is often mechanically weak, making it susceptible to removal under applied loads, leading to material loss. Stack and El Badia [16] investigated the erosion-corrosion behaviour of WC-CoCr coatings deposited by the HVOF process. They found that preferential corrosion at the binder-carbide interface led to increased mass loss during erosion-corrosion tests. Thakare et al. [17] used in-situ electrochemical measurements to calculate material loss of WC-10Co-4Cr coatings subjected to simultaneous mechanical and electrochemical damage. They found that the material loss due to pure wear was two to three orders of magnitude higher than the electrochemical wear.

Given that the corrosion mechanism associated with WC-based metal matrix coatings is itself a complex phenomenon due to the heterogeneous microstructure, the underlying mechanism in tribocorrosion conditions is even more intricate. This complexity necessitates assessing the corrosion and tribocorrosion performance of the coatings to have better insights into the degradation behaviour. In line with the above challenge, the present study aims to investigate the corrosion and tribocorrosion behaviour of HVAF-deposited WC-based coatings with a new sustainable iron-based binder, WC-FeCrNiMo, with two different powder sizes. For benchmarking purposes, a standard WC-CoCr system was included for the investigation.

Two WC-FeCrNiMo, Amperit®619 powders with different particle size distributions—fine (25/5 μm) and coarse (45/15 μm)—were employed in this investigation. These powders were supplied by Höganäs AB and produced via the agglomeration and sintering route. For benchmarking purposes, a commercially available coarse WC-10Co4Cr (Amperit®558.074 45/15 μm) and fine WC-10Co4Cr (Amperit®558.059 30/5 μm) were used. Details of the feedstock materials are presented in Table 1 . SS-304 substrates with a diameter of 1 in. and 6 mm thickness were used to deposit the coatings. Prior to deposition, all substrates were degreased and mounted on a carousel rotating to yield a linear speed of 1.66 m/s. It is noteworthy that no grit blasting was performed on the substrates prior to coating. Coating deposition was carried out using an M3 HVAF thermal spray system (Uniquecoat Technologies LLC, Oilville, USA). Propane was used as a fuel gas, while nitrogen served as a carrier gas. A de Laval nozzle with a 5L2 configuration was utilized to deposit all the above powders. The deposited coatings were identified based on the feedstock and powder size. For instance, “WC-Fe/WC-CoCr_Pc/Pf” represents the feedstock (WC-Fe or WC-CoCr) and powder size (Pc for coarse and Pf for fine). The HVAF process parameters utilized in the current study are summarized in Table 2 .

The cross-sectional microstructure of the coating was initially studied using scanning electron microscopy (HITACHI TM3000 tabletop microscope and Carl Zeiss, Gemini). To analyse the cross-sectional microstructure of the coating, the samples were first sectioned using a diamond blade, then hot-mounted, followed by grinding and polishing. The metallographic preparation of the coating involved sequential grinding with a 45 μm diamond disk, then polishing using 9 and 3 μm Kemet diamond slurries. Finally, mirror-polishing of the samples was achieved using a colloidal silica MasterMet 2 suspension.

Phase analysis of the coatings was performed using a high-resolution X-ray Diffractometer (X'Pert PRO, PANalytical, Netherlands) with a source emitting Cu-Kα X-rays of wavelength 1.5406 Å operating at 30 kV. The coatings were scanned over the Bragg diffraction angle ranging from 20° to 90° with a step size of 0.017°. For comparison, the XRD pattern of the respective powders was included in the study.

The corrosion behaviour of the coatings was studied using a potentiostat (Gamry Reference 3000) equipped with a conventional three-electrode cell system. An Ag/AgCl electrode serves as a reference, a graphite block as a counter electrode, and the coating acts as the working electrode. The corrosion test was conducted in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The area of the specimen exposed to the solution was 1.855 cm2. Initially, open circuit potential (OCP) was applied for 1 h to stabilize the potential, followed by a potentiodynamic polarization scan varying from −200 mV to 1000 mV vs Ag/AgCl with respect to OCP. The potential sweep rate was set at 0.5 mV/s. The corrosion test was repeated three times for reproducibility.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was also performed to study the coating surface-electrolyte interface reaction. The EIS test was conducted with an amplitude of 10 mV sinusoidal voltage superimposed on OCP. The frequency was set in the range of 104 to 10−1 Hz with a sampling rate of 10 points per decade. This frequency range and sampling rate yield stable EIS results. Finally, both the Nyquist and Bode plots were analyzed and fitted with an equivalent electrical circuit.

The tribocorrosion behaviour of the coatings was investigated using a custom-made setup. The tribometer, featuring a ball-on-flat configuration (Ducom Instruments, India), was integrated with a potentiostat (GillAC, ACM Instruments, UK) in a three-electrode cell system. A standard calomel electrode (SCE) served as the reference electrode, a graphite rod functioned as the counter electrode, and the coating acted as the working electrode. The exposed area of the specimen measured 1 cm2, while the remaining area was masked with an acrylic coating. An alumina ball with a 6 mm diameter was selected as the counterpart. The load was set at 20 N, and the stroke length was 0.5 mm with a frequency of 5 Hz. The wear track following the tribocorrosion test was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss, Gemini) and a 3D optical profilometer (3D Proflim). The wear volume loss was calculated from the 3D optical profilometer image. In this study, the tribocorrosion behaviour of the coatings was analyzed in two modes: 1) open circuit potential (OCP) and 2) potentiostatic mode. A set of specific protocols was followed for OCP measurements (Fig. 1 ): 1) The specimen was immersed in the electrolyte for 900 s for stabilization. During this stage, the counterpart was not in contact with the specimen; 2) around 800 s, the counterpart was made to contact with specimen, and load was applied; 3) at 900 s, the coatings were subjected to sliding motion for 3600 s, 4) after 5400 s, the sliding was stopped, and the load was removed, 5) again the OCP was recorded for 900 s to repassivate. The OCP was recorded at 1 point per second throughout the test. In potentiostatic mode, the potential remained constant, and the current was measured with time. An anodic potential of +250 mV was chosen from the potentiodynamic polarization graph. The procedure followed for the potentiostatic study was similar to the above OCP study. Fig. 1 shows the procedure followed for both OCP and potentiostatic studies.

Fig. 2 illustrates the cross-sectional morphology of fine and coarse WC-FeCrNiMo and WC-CoCr powders. Both feedstock materials exhibit a predominantly spherical morphology, which is characteristic of agglomerated and sintered powders. Furthermore, the microstructure reveals that both fine and coarse powders exhibit metal binder agglomeration (represented by the dark grey phase) along with the presence of pores. Fig. 3 shows the cross-sectional SEM images of WC-Fe_Pf, WC-Fe_Pc, WC-CoCr_Pc, and WC-CoCr_Pf coatings. All the coatings show a dense microstructure with a homogenous distribution of carbides and binders, along with the presence of metal binder agglomeration. It should be noted that the agglomerated metal binder is directly carried forward from the feedstock, as the HVAF processes retain the original microstructure of the feedstock to a large extent.

The powder size is seen to have a notable influence on the deposition rate of the coatings. For instance, the deposition rate (calculated based on the thickness built up per pass) of WC-Fe_Pf was lower than that of WC-Fe_Pc; a similar trend was also observed for the WC-CoCr system, as shown in Fig. 3 (i). Torkashvand et al. [18] also observed a similar trend where the deposition rate was lower for the coatings deposited with fine WC-CoCr (20/5 μm) compared to coarser WC-CoCr (45/15 μm), regardless of nozzle configuration. The lower deposition rate of fine particles can be attributed to the flattening behaviour and powder rebound during deposition. For example, suppose the carbide size remains the same, and the powder particle size is decreased. In that case, the flattening ability of the powder decreases progressively, and the chance of powder rebound increases [19]. In addition, it has also been reported that higher particle velocity leads to higher rebound of particles. As the particle size decreases, the velocity increases, which may lead to a greater likelihood of rebound, thereby influencing the deposition rate [20]. A prior study from this group previously measured the velocity of in-flight coarse, medium, and fine WC-CoCr powders using AccuraSpray-G3C–G3C (Tecnar, Quebec, Canada) for different nozzle configurations. They found that the fine WC-CoCr powder traverses ∼200 m/s higher than a corresponding coarser powder [18]. Similarly, in the present study, the fine powders traverse faster than the coarser powders, leading to higher rebound, thereby resulting in a lower deposition rate. It should also be noted that the particle size and velocity are not the only criteria that dictate the deposition rate; other factors, such as powder porosity, primary WC size, apparent density, and base material conditions, also influence the deposition rate.

XRD analysis of WC-Fe_Pf, WC-Fe_Pc, WC-CoCr_Pc, and WC-CoCr_Pf coatings and their corresponding powders was performed to study the phases present in the coating. Both WC-FeCrNiMo and WC-CoCr powders exhibit the major WC phase along with faint peaks from 40 to 45°, which corresponds to the η-phases. In the case of coatings, most of the major phase composition of the feedstock powder was retained, as shown in Fig. 4 . Most importantly, the presence of the W2C phase was negligible for both the coatings deposited with WC-FeCrNiMo and WC-CoCr powders, suggesting minimal decarburization during the HVAF processes. In addition to the above, a noticeable peak broadening was observed for all the coatings compared to the feedstock powder, which is attributed to plastic deformation upon solid-state compaction.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves for both fine and coarse WC-FeCrNiMo, as well as the WC-CoCr system, are illustrated in Fig. 5a, while the evaluation of passivation current density (Ipp) is depicted in Fig. 5b. As can be seen from the polarization curves, a clear distinction between WC-Fe_Pf and WC-Fe_Pc coatings was observed. For instance, the WC-Fe_Pf coating, processed with fine powder, exhibits better corrosion resistance, particularly at the anodic potential, which shows a well-defined passivation plateau with a low passivation current density (Ipp) (0.032 × 10−3 A/cm2) compared to the WC-Fe_Pc coating (2.97 × 10−3 A/cm2). In general, the presence of a passivation plateau indicates the formation of a compact and defect-free passive layer. In the present case, this could plausibly be the formation of a Cr2O3 oxide layer that protects the coating surface. In contrast, despite identical binder chemistry, such kind of a passivation plateau was not observed in the WC-Fe_Pc coating; moreover, the Ipp was found to be two orders of magnitude higher. A similar trend was also observed for the WC-CoCr system, where the WC-CoCr_Pf coating exhibits better corrosion resistance by possessing a lower Ipp (0.29 × 10−3 A/cm2) than the WC-CoCr_Pc coating (2.24 × 10−3 A/cm2). However, the passivation plateau was not observed in both cases, which further evinces the influence of binder chemistry. Overall, the fine WC-Fe_Pf coating possesses better corrosion resistance among all the coatings.

In addition to potentiodynamic polarization analysis, the EIS analysis was employed to study the electrochemical interface of the coatings in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the form of a Nyquist plot (Fig. 6a), Bode phase modulus plot (Fig. 6b), and Bode phase angle plot (Fig. 6c). In the Nyquist plot, the WC-Fe_Pf coating exhibited a larger diameter followed by WC-CoCr_Pf coating, suggesting an enhancement in the corrosion resistance. In contrast, both WC-Fe_Pc and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings show a depressed arc with a lower diameter. Particularly, the WC-CoCr_Pc coatings show two time constants. From the Bode impedance plot, it is observed that the WC-Fe_Pf coating exhibits a higher absolute impedance value |Z| compared to all the coatings. Though the Bode phase angle for all the coatings shows inflections at the lower frequencies, both WC-Fe_Pf and WC-CoCr_Pf coatings showed a stable barrier across wider frequencies compared to WC-Fe_Pc and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings.

Both the Nyquist and Bode plots were fitted with equivalent electrical circuits, which are shown in Fig. 6d, and their corresponding values were tabulated in Table 3 . These equivalent circuits show a good correlation between the measured and the fitted curve, with the smallest error of fit. The inset in Fig. 6(a, b, c) shows the raw and fitted data of WC-Fe_Pf coating to demonstrate the fitteness with an equivalent electrical circuit shown in Fig. 6d. In the circuit, Rs represents electrolyte/solution resistance, which relaxes at higher frequencies. Qc & Rpo are associated with constant phase elements and pore resistance, respectively, and represent the reactions at the porous passive film at higher frequencies. Qdl & Rct are the double-layer phase element capacitance and charge transfer resistance, respectively, and constitute the reaction at the compact passive film at lower frequencies. In the present study, the origin of symbol Q signifies the existence of non-ideal capacitance behaviour (CPE, constant phase element) due to the fractal geometry, such as surface heterogeneities. The impedance of CPE (ZCPE) is a function of the angular frequency and is related to that for a capacitance by the equation,
where j2 = −1 and n is an empirical exponent between 0 and 1. For a capacitor, n = 1; for a resistor, n = 0, and for an inductor, n = −1.

The EIS technique was implemented to better understand the mechanism at the electrode-electrolyte interface. In the Nyquist plot, the presence of a semicircle or capacitive arc signifies the RC circuit (Rpo & Cc), indicating the formation of a passive layer. This arc correlates with the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which ultimately defines the corrosion resistance based on its relative diameter. A larger diameter indicates increased corrosion resistance. The larger diameter of the WC-Fe_Pf coating, followed by the WC-CoCr_Pf, signifies enhanced corrosion resistance, while the WC-Fe_Pc and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings demonstrate a depressed arc. The Bode phase-angle plot assesses the material's protective efficacy. Ideally, the phase angle for pure capacitance is 90° and should remain stable at lower frequencies. However, in Fig. 6c, the phase angle for all coatings was below 90° and exhibited inflection at lower frequencies, which is likely due to surface inhomogeneity. Nevertheless, when comparing the coatings, the WC-Fe_Pf demonstrates a relatively higher phase angle than the others, implying its enhanced corrosion resistance. The Bode impedance plot shows that the modulus |Z| value at lower frequencies reflects the oxide layer's ability to impede current flow between anodic and cathodic sites. In this context, the WC-Fe_Pf coating presents the highest modulus |Z| compared to the WC-Fe_Pc and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings, confirming the presence of a dense oxide layer that serves as a barrier and improves corrosion resistance. The EIS analysis results align well with the potentiodynamic polarization studies, where the coatings deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo exhibited a clearly defined passivation plateau at elevated anodic potentials.

The corrosion mechanism involved in thermal spray WC-based coatings is complex due to the heterogeneous nature of the coating structure. The carbide and metal binder represent a heterogeneous material system, with both components being conductive; the electrical resistivity of carbide is 100 μΩ cm, and that of the metal binder is 5 μΩ cm, resulting in galvanic coupling between the two phases [6]. As a consequence, this galvanic coupling between the two phases will force the anodic part of the overall corrosion reaction to proceed on the metallic binder, thereby resulting in preferential corrosion of the metallic binder [21–23]. Souza and Neville [24,25] compared the corrosion performance of UNSS31603 stainless steel with that of HVOF WC-10Co-4Cr in artificial seawater. They found that, unlike the continuous passive film formed on the stainless-steel sample, the HVOF sample only exhibited oxide islands on the binder. This behaviour was also noted by Takeda et al. [26], where they observed patchy Cr2O3 oxide formation on WC-10Co-4Cr when immersed in a Na2SO4 solution. This oxide layer is likely weakest near the binder-carbide interface, providing further insight into the increased corrosion at this junction. Similarly, in the present study, the SEM images (shown in Fig. 7 ) of the corroded areas of all the coatings reveal preferential corrosion of the binder, corroborated well by the findings of the aforementioned studies. In particular, the WC-Fe_Pc coating shows extensive corrosion of the binder and correlates well with polarization curves, as it possesses a higher passivation current density at higher anodic potential. In contrast, the extent of binder corrosion for the WC-Fe_Pf coating was less pronounced compared to the WC-Fe_Pc coating. This observation correlates well with polarization analysis, where the WC-Fe_Pf exhibits a passivation plateau with low passivation current density. Similarly, in the case of the WC-CoCr system, the WC-CoCr_Pf coating reveals relatively less pronounced binder corrosion than the WC-CoCr_Pc coating. Overall, the above results suggest that both powder size and binder chemistry play a significant role in influencing corrosion behaviour. Notably, the fine WC-FeCrNiMo presented better corrosion resistance among all the coatings. A plausible explanation for the enhanced corrosion resistance of coatings deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo may be due to the pronounced peening effect, as powder size is the only factor that had a prominent influence. Typically, the HVAF technique, characterized by high particle velocity and strong kinetic impact, results in a substantial peening effect during deposition, which in turn generates higher compressive residual stresses in the coatings. For example, Varis et al. reported that the WC-CoCr coating deposited using high kinetic thermal spray processes such as Kermetico AK7 (HVAF) and Thermio CJS (HVOF) generates higher compressive stress than the traditional DJ Hybrid HVOF process [27]. Furthermore, the fine powders induce a more peening effect compared to the coarser ones. As already discussed in Section 3.1, the fine powder results in more pronounced rebounding, thereby resulting in a lower deposition rate. Due to this, fine powder requires more passes to achieve the target thickness and consequently leads to a more peening effect. In the present study, we hypothesize that enhanced corrosion resistance of WC-Fe_Pf coating might be associated with higher compressive residual stresses induced by the pronounced peening effect. For instance, it has been reported that the compressive residual stress generated during peening can reduce interatomic distances and the thickening of Cr atoms at the surface, which facilitates the construction, growth, and maintenance of the oxide layer [28]. Besides compressive stress, peening may also result in increased dislocation density [29]. Similarly, the pronounced peening effect might result in increased dislocation density in the metallic binder. This increased dislocation density can provide more active sites, potentially facilitating the spontaneous formation of a passive layer that protects the surface. Nevertheless, given the complexity of the underlying mechanisms, further investigations are required to elucidate the relationship between peening-induced residual stresses and their influence on corrosion behaviour.

The tribocorrosion behaviour of the coatings at OCP in a 3.5 wt% solution is shown in Fig. 8 . The figure illustrates characteristic potential behaviour, that is, before, during, and after sliding motion. Before sliding, the potential of all the coatings was observed to shift towards a noble direction, indicating the formation of a passive layer during these periods. Specifically, the potential of coatings deposited with WC-FeCrNiMo powder exhibits a nobler potential than that of WC-CoCr, highlighting the influence of binder chemistry on the formation of the passive layer. In the next phase, when the sliding starts, interestingly, no significant potential drop was noticed for all the coatings. In fact, the potential of the WC-CoCr_Pf coating was increasing during sliding. Only in the case of the WC-Fe_Pf coating, the potential was found to be continuously shifted towards a negative value throughout the sliding motion. Despite this negative shift, the overall potential of the WC-Fe_Pf coating remained positive among all the coatings. In the final phase, i.e., when the sliding stops, a small increase in potential was observed for the WC-Fe_Pf and WC-CoCr_Pf coatings, implying the ability to recover the passive layer after the sliding stops. The above findings are similar to the HVOF-sprayed WC-10Co4Cr, WC-12Co, and WC-CrC-12Ni coatings tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution [30].

Fig. 9 shows the evolution of current density over time for all the coatings. For the potentiostatic studies, a constant potential of +250 mV was selected based on the anodic region observed in the potentiodynamic graph (Fig. 4a), where the transition from the active to passive region begins at this potential, which is considered the passive region for all coatings. Similar to OCP, the potentiostatic mode also illustrates current behaviour in three regions: before, during, and after sliding motion. At the start of the potentiostatic test, the current was initially higher but decayed within a few seconds, indicating non- faradaic charging and diffusion- controlled faradaic reactions. During sliding, no sharp current increase was observed across all coatings, except for WC-Fe_Pc, where the current increased during sliding motion. Notably, current behaviour in the potentiostatic test differs from that in potentiodynamic polarization; for example, WC- Fe_Pf showed lower current density than WC- CoCr in polarization, but this trend reversed in the tribocorrosion test.

Generally, passive materials tend to show significant potential drop and increased current during sliding due to passive layer rupture, exposing active metal and causing galvanic coupling between worn and unworn areas. In cermet coatings, this mechanism is complex due to their heterogeneous microstructure, with carbides and metal binders already forming galvanic couples, adding further complexity. A plausible explanation for the observed potential and current behaviour, especially in WC-Fe_Pf, WC-CoCr_Pf, and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings, relates to the contact geometry between sliding faces. For instance, Bratu et al. [31]. explained potential drop and current increases during fretting corrosion based on the real contact area (Ar).

where k is a constant, F is the normal force, and m = 1 (plastic deformation) or 0. 0.67 (elastic deformation). An increase in Ar can remove oxide films from larger areas, exposing more active material, lowering the potential and increasing the anodic current. In this case, we believe the 20 N normal force was insufficient to remove the oxide layer over a larger area, thus not inducing significant wear or changes in potential and current. This aligns with our previous work, where coatings tested at 20 N over 5000 m under dry conditions exhibited extremely low specific wear rates (∼10−8 mm 3/Nm) [32,33]. Given the complexity of tribocorrosion and the microstructural heterogeneity of thermal- sprayed cermet coatings, further research is needed to better understand these mechanisms.

The worn surface morphology after the potentiostatic test was examined using SEM, as shown in Fig. 10 . As seen in the images, all the coatings display a tribo-oxide layer within the wear tracks, indicating an oxidative wear mechanism [34]. In order to have better insights into the chemistry of the tribo-oxide layer, EDS mapping was performed inside the wear track region, shown in Fig. 11 . The EDS mapping confirms that the tribo-oxide consists mainly of oxidized elements, particularly Cr, along with contributions from Al originating from the counterbody (alumina). Additionally, we also observe crack formation in all the coatings (predominantly occurring only in the tribo-oxide layer), which is likely the consequence of mechanical stress and fatigue during sliding. For example, when the thickness of the tribo-oxide layer reaches a critical level, internal stress accumulates, leading to fracture and delamination. Conversely, the short sliding distance (0.5 mm stroke length) used in this study might cause repeated cyclic loading, promoting the initiation and growth of surface and subsurface cracks [34,35]. Although the cracks were observed in all the coatings, their density is considerably lower for WC-Fe_Pf, WC-CoCr_Pf, and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings compared to the WC-Fe_Pc coating. Moreover, the delamination was more pronounced in the WC-Fe_Pc coating compared to the other coatings, which exhibit less delamination and maintain relatively smoother wear tracks. The above morphological findings correlate well with the potentiostatic response, where the current of WC-Fe_Pc coating was higher and also increased during the sliding motion, consistent with its higher crack density and delaminations. In contrast, the current density almost remains unchanged for WC-Fe_Pf, WC-CoCr_Pf, and WC-CoCr_Pc coatings.

Another significant finding from the potentiostatic test was that the volume loss was directly influenced by powder size. For example, the volume loss evaluated from the 3D optical profilometer, as shown in Fig. 12 , reveals that the WC-Fe_Pf coating was found to be comparable to that of the WC-CoCr_Pc coating, which is quite intriguing. For instance, our group has separately demonstrated that the specific wear rate of WC-CoCr_Pc coating was lower than that of WC-Fe_Pf and WC-Fe_Pc coatings when tested at dry conditions [32,33]. This contrasting finding can be directly attributed to the passivating ability of coatings deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powder. As already observed in cyclic polarization, the WC-Fe_Pf coating demonstrated a well-defined passivation plateau at higher anodic potential, indicating a compact and defect-free passive layer. Similarly, under dynamic conditions, i.e., the combined action of wear and corrosion, a thick tribo-oxide layer formed, which could restrict direct contact between the counterpart and the coating surface, thereby enhancing protection efficiency.

The present study systematically investigated the corrosion and tribocorrosion performance of WC-FeCrNiMo and WC-CoCr coatings with two different powder sizes deposited by HVAF spraying. The key findings are summarized as follows:
•
All the coatings show a dense microstructure regardless of powder particle size and binder chemistry. The coating deposited with fine WC-FeCrNiMo powders showed a lower deposition rate due to increased particle rebound caused by the higher velocity attained by fine particles during the HVAF process.
•
XRD analysis confirmed the retention of major feedstock phases with minimal W₂C decarburization and slightly broadened peaks due to plastic deformation upon solid-state compaction.
•
The WC-Fe_Pf coating exhibited superior corrosion resistance, characterized by lower Ipp and the presence of a distinctive passivation plateau with lower passivation current density at higher anodic potential. When comparing binder chemistry, WC-FeCrNiMo shows better corrosion resistance properties than WC-CoCr. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) corroborated these findings, WC-Fe_Pf coating displaying higher |Z| values and greater charge transfer resistance (Rct).
•
The tribocorrosion performance of the coatings was influenced by the particle size and binder chemistry. Among the WC-FeCrNiMo coatings, the WC-Fe_Pf coatings revealed a stable electrochemical response during sliding motion by exhibiting constant potential and stable current densities. The worn surface morphology reveals that all the coatings underwent oxidative wear by forming a tribo-oxide layer during sliding motion.

Vasanth Gopal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Antonin Riche: Formal analysis, Data curation. Uva Narayanan: Investigation, Data curation. Praveenkumar Kesavan: Investigation, Data curation. Stefan Björklund: Validation, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Oliver Lanz: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Project administration. Sedigheh Bigdeli: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Project administration. Geetha Manivasagam: Writing – review & editing. Shrikant Joshi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work, the authors used Grammarly software in order to check grammar and rephrase to improve sentence formation. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the published article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely acknowledge the financial support from the Knowledge Foundation project Advanced Coatings for Enhanced Sustainability – ACES (project no. 20220171). Also, the authors would like to extend our acknowledgement to Magnus Sandberg for spraying the coatings. P. K. acknowledges support of the European Union unders the REFRESH-Research Excellence for REgion Sustainability and High-tech Industries project (project no. CZ.10.03.01/00/22/0000048).
